description: Kitchen items, utensils, and cooking vocabulary

items.notes: Pronounced 'KEIT-ti-ö'. The double 't' is pronounced long. Partitive: keittiötä.
examples: Keittiö on siisti. — The kitchen is clean.
Syömme keittiössä. — We eat in the kitchen.

items.notes: Pronounced 'LIE-si'. Can also mean 'range' or 'cooktop'. Partitive: liettä.
examples: Liesi on kuuma. — The stove is hot.
Laitoin kattilan liedelle. — I put the pot on the stove.

items.notes: Pronounced 'UU-ni'. The double 'u' is long. Also means 'furnace'. Partitive: uunia.
examples: Laita uuni päälle. — Turn the oven on.
Kakku on uunissa. — The cake is in the oven.

items.notes: Pronounced 'YÄÄ-kaap-pi'. Compound word: jää (ice) + kaappi (cabinet). Partitive: jääkaappia.
examples: Maito on jääkaapissa. — The milk is in the fridge.
Jääkaappi on tyhjä. — The fridge is empty.

items.notes: Pronounced 'AS-ti-an-PE-su-ko-ne'. Compound: astia (dish) + pesu (wash) + kone (machine). A long but logical compound word.
examples: Laita lautaset astianpesukoneeseen. — Put the plates in the dishwasher.
Astianpesukone on täynnä. — The dishwasher is full.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KAT-ti-la'. Double 't' is pronounced long. Partitive: kattilaa.
examples: Keitän vettä kattilassa. — I'm boiling water in a pot.
Tarvitsen ison kattilan. — I need a big pot.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PAN-nu'. Double 'n' is long. A 'paistinpannu' is specifically a frying pan. Partitive: pannua.
examples: Paista kananmunat pannulla. — Fry the eggs in the pan.
Pannu on kuuma. — The pan is hot.

items.notes: Pronounced 'VEIT-si'. The 'ei' diphthong sounds like 'ay' in 'day'. Partitive: veistä.
examples: Veitsi on terävä. — The knife is sharp.
Anna minulle veitsi. — Give me a knife.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HAA-ruk-ka'. Double 'k' is pronounced long. Partitive: haarukkaa.
examples: Syön haarukalla. — I eat with a fork.
Missä on haarukka? — Where is the fork?

items.notes: Pronounced 'LU-sik-ka'. Double 'k' is pronounced long. Partitive: lusikkaa.
examples: Tarvitsen lusikan soppaa varten. — I need a spoon for the soup.
Sekoita lusikalla. — Stir with a spoon.

items.notes: Pronounced 'LAU-ta-nen'. Follows the -nen noun pattern. Partitive: lautasta.
examples: Lautanen on pöydällä. — The plate is on the table.
Pese lautaset. — Wash the plates.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KUP-pi'. Double 'p' is long. Used for coffee cups and mugs. Partitive: kuppia.
examples: Haluatko kupin kahvia? — Would you like a cup of coffee?
Kuppi on likainen. — The cup is dirty.

items.notes: Pronounced 'LA-si'. Also means 'glass' as a material. Partitive: lasia.
examples: Juon lasin vettä. — I drink a glass of water.
Lasi meni rikki. — The glass broke.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KUL-ho'. Partitive: kulhoa. Used for serving bowls and mixing bowls.
examples: Laita salaatti kulhoon. — Put the salad in a bowl.
Tarvitsen ison kulhon. — I need a big bowl.

items.notes: Pronounced 'LEIK-kuu-lau-ta'. Compound: leikkuu (cutting) + lauta (board). Double 'k' and 'u' are long.
examples: Leikkaa sipuli leikkuulaudalla. — Cut the onion on the cutting board.
Pese leikkuulauta käytön jälkeen. — Wash the cutting board after use.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KEIT-tää'. Double 't' is long. Conjugation: keitän (I cook), keität (you cook), keittää (he/she cooks). Also means 'to boil'.
examples: Keitän perunoita. — I'm cooking potatoes.
Äiti keittää soppa. — Mom is cooking soup.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PAIS-taa'. Conjugation: paistan (I fry), paistat (you fry), paistaa (he/she fries). Used for frying and oven roasting.
examples: Paistan kalaa pannulla. — I'm frying fish in a pan.
Paista liha uunissa. — Roast the meat in the oven.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SE-koit-taa'. Double 't' is long. Conjugation: sekoitan (I stir), sekoitat (you stir). Works for both mixing ingredients and stirring a pot.
examples: Sekoita taikina hyvin. — Mix the dough well.
Sekoitan keittoa. — I'm stirring the soup.

items.notes: Pronounced 'RE-sep-ti'. A loanword, similar to English. Partitive: reseptiä.
examples: Tämä resepti on helppo. — This recipe is easy.
Löysin hyvän reseptin. — I found a good recipe.

items.notes: Pronounced 'MAUS-te'. The 'au' diphthong is like 'ow' in 'how'. Partitive: maustetta.
examples: Lisää mausteita ruokaan. — Add spices to the food.
Tämä mauste on vahva. — This spice is strong.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SUO-la'. The 'uo' diphthong is common in Finnish. Partitive: suolaa.
examples: Lisää suolaa. — Add salt.
Ruuassa on liikaa suolaa. — The food has too much salt.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PIP-pu-ri'. Double 'p' is long. Refers to black pepper. Partitive: pippuria.
examples: Haluatko pippuria? — Would you like pepper?
Lisää pippuria ja suolaa. — Add pepper and salt.

items.notes: Pronounced 'ÖL-jy'. Note both ö and y are front vowels. Partitive: öljyä. 'Oliiviöljy' = olive oil.
examples: Lisää öljyä pannulle. — Add oil to the pan.
Käytän oliiviöljyä. — I use olive oil.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SO-ke-ri'. Partitive: sokeria. A common kitchen staple.
examples: Laita sokeria kahviin. — Put sugar in the coffee.
En käytä sokeria. — I don't use sugar.

items.notes: Pronounced 'JAU-hot'. Usually used in plural form (jauhot). Singular 'jauho' means a single grain of flour. Partitive: jauhoja.
examples: Tarvitsen jauhoja leivontaan. — I need flour for baking.
Lisää jauhoja taikinaan. — Add flour to the dough.
